name: Caribbean Banking FX Crisis — Trinidad & Tobago
tagline: Simulate an FX liquidity crisis in T&T's banking system with USD shortages, capital flight, and CBTT intervention.
scenario_description: Trinidad and Tobago faces a sharp decline in energy revenues following a sustained drop in natural gas and crude oil prices. The resulting USD shortage triggers a widening parallel FX market premium, corporate hoarding of USD, and growing depositor anxiety. The CBTT must balance its managed float regime against dwindling reserves, while the banking sector faces asset quality deterioration in energy-exposed portfolios. The IMF is monitoring but has not yet been formally engaged.

actors.name: Central Bank of Trinidad and Tobago (CBTT)
actors.description: The monetary authority responsible for price stability, FX management, and financial system oversight. Operates a managed float for the TTD. Holds approximately USD 6.3 billion in net official reserves (declining). Must balance inflation targeting with FX market stability and political pressure to maintain the exchange rate.
objectives: Maintain orderly FX market conditions and defend the managed float
Preserve adequate foreign reserve coverage (target: 8+ months of imports)
Ensure financial system stability and prevent bank runs
Contain inflation expectations within 2-6% target band
constraints: Reserves declining at USD 150-200M per quarter
Political pressure to avoid formal devaluation
Limited monetary policy transmission in a small open economy
Must maintain confidence of international rating agencies
available_actions: Adjust repo rate (currently 3.50%)
Intervene in FX market via USD sales to authorised dealers
Impose or tighten FX surrender requirements on energy companies
Issue moral suasion to banks on USD allocation priorities
Activate emergency liquidity assistance facility
Tighten prudential requirements on FX-exposed lending
Coordinate with Ministry of Finance on fiscal response
Engage IMF for technical assistance or precautionary facility

actors.name: Republic Bank Limited
actors.description: The largest commercial bank in T&T by assets (approx. TT$70B), with significant regional operations across the Caribbean. Dominant market share in corporate and retail banking. Energy sector exposure through project finance and corporate lending. Listed on the Trinidad and Tobago Stock Exchange (TTSE). Conservative risk culture with strong capital buffers (CAR ~18%).
objectives: Maintain profitability and shareholder returns (target ROE 15%+)
Preserve USD liquidity for trade finance obligations
Protect asset quality — limit NPL ratio below 4%
Retain corporate depositors and prevent large-scale USD withdrawals
constraints: Energy sector loan book represents ~22% of total lending
USD liquidity depends on CBTT allocation and energy company flows
Regional subsidiaries require USD capital support
Reputation risk from perceived preferential USD allocation
available_actions: Tighten USD credit lines to non-essential importers
Increase TTD deposit rates to attract local currency funding
Accelerate provisioning on energy-exposed loans
Lobby CBTT for preferential USD allocation as systemic bank
Reduce regional subsidiary capital deployment
Offer structured FX products to corporate clients
Engage in informal parallel market to source USD (reputational risk)
Issue public confidence statements to retail depositors

actors.name: First Citizens Group
actors.description: State-owned financial group (Government of T&T holds ~63% via NIB and Treasury). Second largest bank by assets. Dual mandate: commercial profitability and national development objectives. Significant exposure to government and state enterprise lending. Less conservative than Republic Bank; faster growing but thinner capital buffers (CAR ~15%).
objectives: Balance commercial returns with government policy objectives
Maintain competitive position against Republic Bank
Support government's economic diversification agenda
Preserve adequate capital ratios despite potential state-directed lending
constraints: Government ownership creates implicit pressure for directed lending
State enterprise exposure (utilities, energy SOEs) is concentrated
Capital buffers thinner than Republic Bank — less room for shock absorption
Board composition includes government appointees with policy agendas
available_actions: Prioritise USD allocation to government-strategic importers
Request government capital injection if CAR falls below regulatory minimum
Tighten credit standards on private sector energy lending
Coordinate with CBTT on system-wide liquidity support
Adjust pricing on TTD products to improve net interest margin
Accelerate digital banking push to reduce operating costs
Support government bond issuance through primary dealer activities
Expand SME lending as diversification from energy concentration

actors.name: Ministry of Finance — Government of T&T
actors.description: The fiscal authority managing government revenue, expenditure, and debt. Energy revenues (petroleum levy, supplemental petroleum tax, dividends from NGC/Petrotrin successor) historically fund 30-40% of the budget. Heritage and Stabilisation Fund (HSF) holds approximately USD 5.5 billion. Debt-to-GDP ratio ~75%. Must navigate fiscal consolidation while maintaining social stability.
objectives: Close fiscal gap created by energy revenue decline
Avoid drawing down HSF beyond prudent limits
Maintain sovereign credit rating (currently BBB-/stable)
Preserve social spending to maintain political stability
constraints: Energy revenue shortfall of TT$3-5B annually if prices stay depressed
HSF withdrawal rules limit annual drawdown to 60% of under-collection
Public sector wage bill is rigid and politically sensitive
Upcoming election cycle constrains austerity measures
available_actions: Draw on Heritage and Stabilisation Fund within statutory limits
Issue domestic or international sovereign bonds
Implement revenue-side measures (property tax enforcement, VAT widening)
Cut capital expenditure programme while protecting social transfers
Negotiate IMF stand-by arrangement or precautionary credit line
Lobby energy companies for accelerated tax and royalty payments
Announce economic diversification programme to signal confidence
Coordinate fiscal-monetary policy response with CBTT

actors.name: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
actors.description: Multilateral institution monitoring T&T's macroeconomic stability through Article IV consultations. Has not been formally engaged for a programme but is in regular dialogue with CBTT and MOF. Would impose conditionality (FX regime flexibility, fiscal consolidation, structural reforms) in exchange for any financial support. Caribbean Regional Technical Assistance Centre (CARTAC) provides ongoing technical support.
objectives: Promote macroeconomic stability and sustainable growth in T&T
Advocate for exchange rate flexibility to absorb external shocks
Encourage fiscal consolidation and energy revenue diversification
Prevent T&T's situation from creating regional contagion
constraints: Cannot intervene without formal government request
Programme conditionality must follow established frameworks
Politically sensitive in T&T — IMF programme seen as sovereignty loss
Must balance T&T-specific advice with Caribbean-wide stability
available_actions: Publish Article IV staff report with policy recommendations
Offer precautionary Flexible Credit Line (FCL) or Stand-By Arrangement
Provide technical assistance on FX regime modernisation
Issue public statements on T&T economic outlook
Coordinate with CDB and IDB on complementary support
Recommend structural benchmarks (SOE reform, fiscal rules)
Facilitate debt sustainability analysis if requested
Engage in confidential policy dialogue with CBTT Governor

actors.name: Fintech & VASP Sector
actors.description: Emerging fintech and virtual asset service provider (VASP) ecosystem in T&T. Includes crypto exchanges, digital payment platforms, and remittance fintechs. During FX crises, these entities amplify parallel market activity by providing alternative USD access channels (USDT/USDC stablecoin trading, cross-border crypto transfers). Small but growing — estimated USD 50-100M in monthly volumes. Regulatory framework under development by CBTT.
objectives: Capture market share from traditional banks during FX scarcity
Profit from widening parallel market premium (spread arbitrage)
Avoid regulatory crackdown while expanding operations
Position as legitimate financial infrastructure for post-crisis era
constraints: Unregulated or lightly regulated — vulnerable to sudden enforcement
Dependent on international exchange counterparties for liquidity
Limited consumer trust outside tech-savvy demographics
Anti-money-laundering scrutiny could shut down operations
available_actions: Increase USDT/USDC trading volumes as parallel FX channel
Lower fees to attract corporate and retail FX demand
Lobby for regulatory clarity and licensing framework
Partner with regional fintechs for cross-border flows
Self-regulate (KYC/AML compliance) to preempt crackdown
Launch TTD-backed stablecoin product
Provide data on parallel market rates that influences expectations
Attract diaspora remittance flows away from traditional channels

actors.name: Financial Media & Analysts
actors.description: Local and regional financial media (Trinidad Express Business, Guardian Business, CNC3, Loop News), independent economists, and sell-side analysts covering T&T. Their reporting amplifies or dampens depositor confidence and shapes public narrative around the FX situation. International financial press (Bloomberg Caribbean, Reuters) picks up stories that affect sovereign spreads and foreign investor sentiment.
objectives: Break stories on FX market conditions and policy responses
Maintain credibility with readership through accurate reporting
Drive engagement through compelling crisis narrative
Hold institutions accountable for policy decisions
constraints: Access to official data is limited and delayed
Small market — advertising revenue ties to corporate sector goodwill
International outlets amplify local stories unpredictably
Rumour and speculation fill gaps left by official silence
available_actions: Report on parallel market FX rates and spreads
Interview economists and analysts on policy options
Publish investigative pieces on USD allocation patterns
Amplify IMF statements and rating agency reports
Cover depositor behaviour and bank queue reports
Platform opposition criticism of government FX policy
Report corporate earnings that reveal FX impact
Track and publish CBTT reserve data and trends

actors.name: Retail Depositor Segment
actors.description: Approximately 800,000 retail deposit accounts across the T&T banking system. Collectively hold TT$60B+ in savings and fixed deposits. Behaviour driven by confidence signals, media reports, and word-of-mouth. During FX stress, depositors attempt to convert TTD savings to USD, creating additional FX demand pressure. Historical memory of 1980s and 2015 FX shortages influences behaviour.
objectives: Preserve purchasing power of savings against TTD depreciation
Secure USD for travel, education, medical, and import needs
Avoid being caught in a bank failure or deposit freeze
Maintain access to banking services without excessive queuing
constraints: Limited financial sophistication — rely on media and social signals
USD purchase limits imposed by banks (typically USD 200-500/month retail)
Cannot easily access parallel market or crypto channels
Savings concentrated in TTD — limited natural hedge
available_actions: Queue at banks for USD allocation on available days
Move deposits to perceived safer institutions
Increase cash holdings (de-banking behaviour)
Purchase durable goods as inflation hedge
Seek USD through informal parallel market contacts
Reduce discretionary spending in anticipation of crisis
Spread rumours and information through social networks
Petition government representatives for relief

actors.name: Corporate & Institutional Depositors
actors.description: Large corporate and institutional depositors including importers, manufacturers, multinational subsidiaries, and pension funds. Hold significant TTD and USD balances. Importers face acute USD shortage for trade finance. Multinationals seek to repatriate dividends. Pension funds face asset-liability mismatch if TTD depreciates. More sophisticated than retail but equally anxious.
objectives: Secure USD for import payments and prevent supply chain disruption
Hedge TTD exposure through available instruments
Maintain banking relationships that ensure priority USD access
Protect real value of pension and investment portfolios
constraints: Import-dependent businesses face immediate operational risk without USD
Forward FX market in T&T is thin — limited hedging options
Large USD requests trigger CBTT scrutiny and allocation queues
Pension fund investment guidelines restrict offshore allocation
available_actions: Negotiate priority USD allocation with relationship bank
Reduce import volumes or switch to regional suppliers
Delay dividend repatriation to reduce FX demand
Lobby CBTT and MOF through business associations (TTMA, TTCIC)
Build USD inventory when available (hoarding behaviour)
Explore alternative trade finance (letters of credit, supplier credit)
Shift pension allocations toward USD-earning equities on TTSE
Publicly or privately pressure government for policy clarity

actors.name: Foreign Portfolio Investors
actors.description: International institutional investors holding T&T government bonds (sovereign and quasi-sovereign), TTSE-listed equities, and corporate bonds. Estimated foreign holdings of ~USD 2B in government securities. Sensitive to credit rating changes, FX regime shifts, and capital control signals. Flight risk is the primary systemic concern — sudden outflow would exhaust CBTT reserves.
objectives: Protect USD-denominated returns on T&T portfolio
Maintain ability to repatriate capital without FX losses
Avoid being trapped by capital controls or FX rationing
Reassess T&T allocation relative to regional alternatives
constraints: Exit requires USD liquidity that CBTT may not provide at scale
Fire-sale of TTD bonds would depress prices and crystalise losses
Reputational risk of being seen to trigger a crisis by exiting
Investment mandate may restrict rapid reallocation
available_actions: Maintain current positions and monitor developments
Gradually reduce T&T exposure through secondary market sales
Request CBTT USD allocation for bond maturity repatriation
Engage rating agencies to flag concerns about FX regime
Shift new allocations to other Caribbean or EM sovereigns
Hedge TTD exposure through offshore NDF market if available
Privately lobby CBTT Governor for repatriation assurances
Publish research notes that influence international sentiment

relationships.description: CBTT is the prudential regulator and lender of last resort for Republic Bank
CBTT regulates First Citizens; state ownership adds a political dimension
Fiscal-monetary policy coordination; CBTT is operationally independent but politically linked
Interbank lending, payment system linkages, and competitive rivalry
Primary banking relationship for ~40% of retail depositors
Dominant corporate banking franchise with deepest USD allocation pool
Second-largest retail banking franchise; perceived government backing provides confidence
Growing corporate franchise, particularly with government-linked enterprises
Government is majority shareholder; can direct strategy through board appointments
MOF appoints CBTT Governor; fiscal needs create implicit pressure on monetary policy
Regular Article IV dialogue; IMF provides technical assistance and policy advice
Fiscal surveillance and debt sustainability monitoring
Emerging alternative for FX access; low trust but growing during scarcity
Unregulated entities creating parallel FX channels; CBTT considering crackdown
Primary source of information shaping depositor confidence and behaviour
Corporates monitor media but also have direct institutional information channels
International media pickup of local stories influences foreign investor sentiment
Foreign investor repatriation requests create direct FX demand on CBTT reserves
Foreign investors hold Republic Bank equity and use it as local custodian
Business associations lobby CBTT for improved USD allocation mechanisms
Some retail depositors explore fintech/crypto as alternative USD access; mostly tech-savvy younger cohort

shock_scenario.trigger: Natural gas prices fall below USD 2.00/MMBtu for a sustained period, causing NGC to cut supply contracts and energy company tax payments to collapse. Simultaneously, a major refinery maintenance shutdown reduces crude export volumes.

timeline.description: USD scarcity becomes visible. Parallel market premium widens from 10% to 15%. Corporate importers begin queuing for USD. CBTT increases FX market interventions.
Media reports on USD shortage go viral. Retail depositors begin queuing at banks. Fintech/VASP volumes spike. Rating agency places T&T on negative watch. Foreign investors begin reducing exposure.
Parallel rate exceeds 8.50 TTD/USD. CBTT reserves fall below 5 months of import cover. MOF considers HSF drawdown and IMF engagement. First Citizens requires capital support discussions.
Policy response crystallises: either managed devaluation with IMF support, or muddling through with tighter controls. Banking system stress peaks and begins to resolve or deepen.

prediction_questions: Will the CBTT formally adjust the TTD/USD rate within the simulation period?
Will CBTT net reserves fall below USD 4 billion?
Will the Government draw on the Heritage and Stabilisation Fund?
Will any bank require emergency liquidity assistance from CBTT?
Will the sovereign credit rating be downgraded?
Will the Government formally engage the IMF for a programme?
Will the parallel market premium exceed 25%?
Will corporate import disruptions cause visible shortages in retail goods?
